ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai boat noodle soup; closest defensible match is the generic noodle soup food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers noodle soup dishes broadly; no Thailand-specific boat noodle soup code exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Thai boat noodle soup as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Thai pork boat noodles soup is approximately 400 g, comprising broth, rice noodles, pork meatballs, pork slices, and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Pork Boat Noodles (Soup) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Thai pork boat noodles (soup), CR ID F05060129, with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice noodles analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai pork boat noodles soup. GI of 58 is estimated from rice noodles (fresh/boiled) as the primary carbohydrate carrier, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 39.2) / 100 = 22.7, rounded to 22.8 for a 400 g bowl (9.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 4 = 39.2 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 493 mg per 100 g; a 400 g bowl delivers approximately 1972 mg sodium, representing 86% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to very high sodium from fish sauce, dark soy sauce, and pork bone broth; if consumed, request broth on the side and use minimal condiments to reduce sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 9.8 g per 100 g with medium GI (58) from rice noodles; a standard 400 g bowl provides approximately 39 g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 23.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume this dish in controlled portions; the moderate GI of rice noodles and the protein content (4 g per 100 g) help moderate glycaemic response, but total carbohydrate per bowl should be counted within the daily carbohydrate budget and sweet condiments should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (493 mg per 100 g) and pork-derived saturated fat contribute to cardiovascular risk; pork blood and pork meatballs add dietary cholesterol.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to high sodium content and pork-derived saturated fat and cholesterol; if consumed, choose a smaller portion, discard most of the broth, and balance the meal with low-sodium, high-fibre foods.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways; high sodium (493 mg per 100 g) may worsen joint swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise this dish due to pro-inflammatory pork-derived arachidonic acid and high sodium; substituting pork with chicken or tofu and requesting reduced fish sauce and soy sauce can lower inflammatory and sodium burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai pork boat noodles soup (ก๋วยเตี๋ยวเรือหมู, Kuai Tiao Ruea Mu) is a traditional Thai noodle soup dish originating from central Thailand, historically sold from boats along the canals of Bangkok. The dish consists of thin rice noodles (kway teow) served in a rich, dark pork bone broth seasoned with fish sauce, dark soy sauce, dried chilli flakes, and garlic. It is typically garnished with pork meatballs, thin slices of pork, pork blood, bean sprouts, and morning glory (water spinach), then finished with crispy pork crackling and a squeeze of lime. The broth is characteristically bold, savoury, and slightly sweet with a deep umami flavour from the combination of pork bone stock and fermented fish sauce. The texture contrasts soft noodles and tender pork with crunchy bean sprouts and crackling. Per 100 g, the dish provides 75 kcal, 4 g protein, 2.19 g fat, 9.8 g carbohydrate, 2.1 g sugar, 0.6 g dietary fibre, and 493 mg sodium. The high sodium content (493 mg per 100 g; approximately 1972 mg per 400 g bowl) is the primary clinical concern, making this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. The medium glycaemic index (estimated GI 58 from rice noodles) and moderate carbohydrate load make it manageable for Type 2 Diabetes in controlled portions. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or halal diets due to pork and pork blood content.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05060129) with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for rice noodles (boiled), which is the primary carbohydrate carrier in this dish. Thai-language name and cultural context verified against established culinary references.
notes: Fields set to -1 indicate data not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this record: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Thai soup dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry as of 2026. Input cultural tags ('western', 'vegetarian', 'plant_based', 'wholegrain') were incorrect and have been corrected to reflect the actual Thai cuisine classification and pork-containing composition. GI value of 58 is an analogous estimate based on boiled rice noodles per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Thai pork boat noodles soup was found in approved sources. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 400 g standard bowl: (58 × 39.2) / 100 = 22.7 g, reported as 22.8.
